`decode(["string"], test_string, errors='backslashreplace')`. I haven't thought about this too hard, so feel free to push back if that doesn't make sense. 

We'll also want to make sure this isn't a breaking change because then I think we'd have to pin eth-abi in web3 which wouldn't be ideal. I _think_ a breaking change can be avoided if we set the `errors` key to the Python default, which is `strict`.

For reviewers, the general code path here is as follows:

1. Users call [`my_bulk_writer.create(...)`](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L363-L365) (or any of the other CRUD methods)
2. The `BulkWriter` instance [adds this new write operation to an in-progress `BulkWriteBatch` instance](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L388), and finalizes that batch in one of two scenarios:
  a. [There are 20 such pooled write operations](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L267-L277), or
  b. The new write operation [touches a document already involved in a different write operation currently queued](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L385-L386)
3. Once a `BulkWriteBatch` instance is created, it is added to a queue ([`my_bulk_writer._queued_batches`](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L285)), and the first domino in the sending chain is called, [`_send_until_queue_is_empty()`](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L225-L228).
4. This method, `_send_until_queue_is_empty()`, [calls `_request_send()`](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L326), which blocks until all rate-limiting rules agree the batch is able to be sent. Note that we are not yet parallelized, so this properly blocks.
5. The first domino method, `_send_until_queue_is_empty()`, [calls the first method that is parallelized, `_send_ batch()`](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L330). A few notes about this new method:
  a. `_send_batch()` is [optionally (and automatically!) parallelized with a decorator](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L87) that [re-routes execution to `my_bulk_writer._executor`](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L80-L83).
  b. `_send_batch()` routes the RPC's response to [`_process_response()`](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L105), which begins [invoking registered callbacks](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L117-L128) for each write in the batch. All of this work still happens in a different thread via the executor, so the main thread is not held up by any of this work.
6. Now back in `_send_until_queue_is_empty()` and thus, on the main thread, the `BulkWriter` instance [checks to see if the queue is in fact empty, and, if it is not, calls itself](https://github.com/googleapis/python-firestore/blob/325f1992ebf9fdedcd80ec53db04fb20a6997494/google/cloud/firestore_v1/bulk_writer.py#L332-L336).

These are the core ideas behind the current implementation, but a few more moving parts are added in the next PR which allow for error handling and retries.

> latest_upstream_materialization_times is going to be relative to the record that we're evaluating this minutes_late value for

I think this might be different than the way I was thinking about it.

Suppose we've got A -> B, with `FreshnessPolicy.maximum_latency(5)` on B.  A materializes at 9:00 am and B materializes at 9:04 am. Then A materializes again at 9:06 am, and now it's 10:00 am and we still haven't gotten any materializations for B.  I would say that B is 54 minutes late, even though the latest materialization for B was within the maximum latency of the latest materialization of A at the time that B materialized.

I had a look at the PR and have some suggestions, especially for the placeholder part:
- If you use the procedure to get the values for the placeholder, rather than the inputs, you can also use this tool together with the sequencer-widget.
- I think it could be a good idea to use the built-in `str.format` function of python to do the actual replacement, giving some additional control/flexibility in the formatting of the values.
- Would it not be a good idea to use the `name` of the `Parameters`, rather than adding a `placeholder` argument? I think this keeps the actual usage more simple, as the `name` is also what's showed on the input-widget.
With these suggestions in mind I toyed around with an implementation, for which I opened a PR (hope you don't mind): #439. I invite you to have a look and would like to hear your thoughts about it/the suggestions.

Haven’t had much time to look at this lately (and probably won’t until the end of summer), but here’s an idea to address the TODO you asked for feedback on (collecting the built instances). The idea is to have the owner of the list allocate it, and have the step builder cooperate and fill that list.

The suggestion also starts taking care of the incompatibility between post generation hooks and this new feature, which will probably be necessary to land it.

```diff
diff --git a/factory/builder.py b/factory/builder.py
index ed39ebc..464a680 100644
--- a/factory/builder.py
+++ b/factory/builder.py
@@ -208,14 +208,18 @@ class BuildStep:
             parent_chain = ()
         return (self.stub,) + parent_chain
 
-    def recurse(self, factory, declarations, force_sequence=None):
+    def recurse(self, factory, declarations, force_sequence=None, collect_instances=None):
         from . import base
         if not issubclass(factory, base.BaseFactory):
             raise errors.AssociatedClassError(
                 "%r: Attempting to recursing into a non-factory object %r"
                 % (self, factory))
         builder = self.builder.recurse(factory._meta, declarations)
-        return builder.build(parent_step=self, force_sequence=force_sequence)
+        return builder.build(
+            parent_step=self,
+            force_sequence=force_sequence,
+            collect_instances=collect_instances,
+        )
 
     def __repr__(self):
         return f"<BuildStep for {self.builder!r}>"
@@ -235,9 +239,8 @@ class StepBuilder:
         self.strategy = strategy
         self.extras = extras
         self.force_init_sequence = extras.pop('__sequence', None)
-        self.created_instances = []
 
-    def build(self, parent_step=None, force_sequence=None):
+    def build(self, parent_step=None, force_sequence=None, collect_instances=None):
         """Build a factory instance."""
         # TODO: Handle "batch build" natively
         pre, post = parse_declarations(
@@ -246,13 +249,6 @@ class StepBuilder:
             base_post=self.factory_meta.post_declarations,
         )
 
-        # TODO: come up with a better solution
-        if parent_step:
-            if hasattr(parent_step, 'builder'):
-                self.created_instances = parent_step.builder.created_instances
-            else:
-                self.created_instances = parent_step.created_instances
-
         if force_sequence is not None:
             sequence = force_sequence
         elif self.force_init_sequence is not None:
@@ -275,21 +271,22 @@ class StepBuilder:
             kwargs=kwargs,
         )
 
-        postgen_results = {}
-        for declaration_name in post.sorted():
-            declaration = post[declaration_name]
-            postgen_results[declaration_name] = declaration.declaration.evaluate_post(
+        if collect_instances is None:
+            postgen_results = {}
+            for declaration_name in post.sorted():
+                declaration = post[declaration_name]
+                postgen_results[declaration_name] = declaration.declaration.evaluate_post(
+                    instance=instance,
+                    step=step,
+                    overrides=declaration.context,
+                )
+            self.factory_meta.use_postgeneration_results(
                 instance=instance,
                 step=step,
-                overrides=declaration.context,
+                results=postgen_results,
             )
-        self.factory_meta.use_postgeneration_results(
-            instance=instance,
-            step=step,
-            results=postgen_results,
-        )
-
-        self.created_instances.append(instance)
+        else:
+            collect_instances.append(instance)
 
         return instance
 
diff --git a/factory/django.py b/factory/django.py
index 25d6966..541e19b 100644
--- a/factory/django.py
+++ b/factory/django.py
@@ -246,13 +246,12 @@ class DjangoModelFactory(base.Factory):
                 "is either not set or False." % dict(f=cls.__name__))
 
         models_to_return = []
-        instances_created = []
+        instances = []
         for _ in range(size):
             step = builder.StepBuilder(cls._meta, kwargs, enums.BUILD_STRATEGY)
-            models_to_return.append(step.build())
-            instances_created.extend(step.created_instances)
+            models_to_return.append(step.build(collect_instances=instances))
 
-        for model_cls, objs in dependency_insert_order(instances_created):

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [ ] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [ ] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

This looks like it should work, but I'm worried that it leaves our internal representation of WDL types a bit half-refactored: we represent pair types and map types with Python objects whose structure mirrors the structure of the types, but we don't do the same for optionals and arrays, and all the terminal types are still strings.

I think all WDL types ought to be represented as `WDLType` subclasses, even terminal ones, and `WDLType` shouldn't extend `str`.

(If we were really clever we could make the `WDLType` subclasses metaclasses, so they could be the types of the objects we pass around at runtime, but that might be impossible because, among other problems, calling `WDLArrayType(WDLIntType)` is going to want to produce a new, distinct type every time you write it. So we probably will have to stick with using `list`, `dict`, and a couple special classes like `WDLPair`.)

I also don't understand the `.` vs `_` hack and why it's needed or why it works.

A note regarding the API JSON response handling in _get_gateway() function:

I'm directly accessing the response body json as well as the response headers, and i'm using strings to do so. 

I think we should eventually have a standardized cloudapi json processing layer/process, where raw json responses would be serialized into the respective VCD object (Network here), and then all other code would just use the Network object. This way if VCD API makes a breaking change, the error & fix would be localized to that json processing layer, instead of in random functions like _get_gateway(). 

However, there seems to be a majority dependence on pyvcloud constructs which use query api (xml resources), so i'm not sure how necessary a processing layer + model classes would be. Either way I think that's out of scope, and this implementation is sufficient and avoids further code clutter.

To save code, I think all of the assignments "aRxInfo.mClass = RxInfo::kPeerMessage;" could be deleted and replaced by a single one of this assignment on top where the aRxInfo instance is initialized. This should work since all of the Handle...() methods for particular MLE message types are private members and all the Handle..() methods are only called from HandleUdpReceive().

This generally looks good, thanks! I haven't verified that it covers all `SDL_GetError()` calls, but it's definitely an improvement.

As a general comment, you've added a bunch of `SDL_ClearError()` calls that are harmless but probably unnecessary. If you are using the recommended pattern, like this pseudocode:

```
ret = SDL_Something()
if ret indicates failure:
    error "{SDL_GetError()}"
```

then there's no need to call `SDL_ClearError()` before `SDL_Something()`. The only time you really need `SDL_ClearError()` is when a function doesn't tell you whether it succeeded or not, like this pseudocode:

```
SDL_ClearError()
SDL_SomeFunctionReturningVoid()
error = SDL_GetError()
if error:
    warning "perhaps SDL_SomeFunctionReturningVoid() failed, or perhaps this is harmless, I can't really tell: {error}"
```

to minimize the chance that the error indicator is still set for some unrelated reason.

So I generally think that boolean flags like this should be avoided. This leads future users of `get_resource_args` to ask: should I error aggressively? Why would I not check errors? Etc etc. In this case my interpretation is that you can only setting `err_aggressively=True` near entry points.

Instead I would recommend having a separate function (`validate_resource_annotatable_function` ?)  that does this type of error checking, and do it ASAP (e.g in the relevant `__init__` or immediately after the user passes the `fn` to the framework) and leave `get_resource_args` unmodified  Alternatively a separate function which does this more extensive error checking and then in turn calls `get_resource_args` that is named appropriately.

I tried it out and ran into a few bugs, most likely minor, and added them to the testing suite as `test_more`.

I also made a modification to the repr format:

```
<Array [2021-06-03T10:00, ... 2021-06-03T13:00] type='4 * datetime64'>
```

and

```
<Array [60 minutes, 60 minutes, 60 minutes] type='3 * timedelta64'>
```

The string representation of datetime/timedelta types don't agree with [Datashape](https://datashape.readthedocs.io/en/latest/types.html#date-time-and-datetime), though. Is it possible to fix this? For one thing, the type strings should drop the "64", but is there a way to get timezone information in datetime (e.g. `datetime[tz='UTC']`)?

There is one significant problem, though: whenever we get data from a datetime/timedelta NumpyArray into an `np.ndarray`, it's through an expensive-looking loop in Python. This should be a zero-copy view. The NumpyArray class already has methods like `to_cupy` and `to_jax` that circumvent the buffer interface (and have to be called explicitly by the code layer above it): we need a similar one for `to_numpy` that would be called when the dtype is not something that can be expressed in a buffer.

Actually, we can short-cut this: I created a `view_int64` property on NumpyArray, which just drops the datetime/timedelta metadata so that you can send it through a buffer and add the metadata back in:

```python
>>> akarray
<Array [2021-06-03T10:00, 2021-06-03T11:00] type='2 * datetime64'>
>>> akarray.layout
<NumpyArray format="M8[m]" shape="2" data="0x 78ad9c01 00000000 b4ad9c01 00000000" at="0x562eaa6b1e10"/>
>>> akarray.layout.view_int64
<NumpyArray format="l" shape="2" data="27045240 27045300" at="0x562eaa6b1e10"/>
>>> np.asarray(akarray.layout.view_int64)
array([27045240, 27045300])
>>> np.asarray(akarray.layout.view_int64).view(akarray.layout.format)
array(['2021-06-03T10:00', '2021-06-03T11:00'], dtype='datetime64[m]')
```

Use this to fix the two Python loops over array data.

There are couple of other configs that I highly recommend to be enabled on a BR  (or router) style device/products:
- For cache table
  -  Suggest increasing `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_TMF_ADDRESS_CACHE_MAX_SNOOP_ENTRIES`  as well (helps allow more of the 128 to be used for snoop optimization)
  - Also the default value for `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_TMF_ADDRESS_QUERY_MAX_RETRY_DELAY`  is rather long (8 hours which I think is given in spec). We typically use much shorter values in internal projects/products.
 

Other feature to enable on routers/BRs:
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_SEND_LINK_REQUEST_ON_ADV_TIMEOUT` 
  - This helps make the links between router a lot more reliable).
- I also highly recommend `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CHILD_SUPERVISION_ENABLE` on router (and on SEDs). 
- And increasing number of message buffers (or heap size if heap is being used for msg buffers).

On SED type devices, I recommend:
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_INFORM_PREVIOUS_PARENT_ON_REATTACH`
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_ATTACH_BACKOFF_ENABLE` (which is enabled by default).

This is awesome :star:.

It unifies `<orm>_get_or_create()` under a single option. Having the `unique_constraints` matching Django `unique_together` facilitates writing `unique_constraints` for Django. `unique_constraints` are more powerful than `get_or_create`, they grant more controls over the lookup strategy. Removing the junk data generated when resolving the factory declarations is just great.

I’m sure users will be thankful for the very clear migration path. It would be nice to provide a deadline for removing the compatibility schemes for `get_or_create`. Perhaps for version 4.0?

IMO, the following commits should be squashed. They all have a single purpose, adding the `unique_constraints` feature. Splitting them was helpful for the review :+1:.

- Expose memleak in SQLAlchemyModelFactory.
- Add an optional normalization to class Meta fields
- Provide support for migrating class Meta fields
- Add `unique_constraints` and associated _lookup()
- Replace *_get_or_create with unique_constraints.

---

As far as I’m concerned, `get_or_create` (or `unique_constraints`) should not be used in tests. When writing a test, the exact state of the database should be known and the dev should know whether the data is to be created or if it’s already present in the DB and should be retrieved. But users rely on that feature, and getting it an upgrade is likely beneficial to them.

---

Commit “Expose memleak in SQLAlchemyModelFactory.”
```diff
-Expose memleak in SQLAlchemyModelFactory.
-Expose memleak in SQLAlchemyModelFactory
```

Commit “Replace *_get_or_create with unique_constraints.”:
```diff
-Replace *_get_or_create with unique_constraints.
+Replace *_get_or_create with unique_constraints
```
and

There is one general issue with "test-case parametrizations" such as in [ogc module](https://github.com/sentinel-hub/sentinelhub-py/blob/19b82df5cc27d31ed36cff212a84cdf00028b2e6/tests/test_ogc.py#L50). Classes `OgcRequest`, `WmsReqest`, `WcsRequest`, `AwsRequest`, etc. actually already make quite some work at the initialization. They prepare a list of requests for files that will have to be downloaded. To create these lists they even have to make some small info requests to e.g. WFS service. This means that before tests start officially running a lot of things already happens (which you can also see from the delay when you execute pytest command). If during this time something would fail, e.g some service would be unavailable or we made some bug, then everything would fail without a good error message.

To improve this, all sh-py classes would have to be initialized during actual test execution. A trick how to do this might be that you replace all class initializations, e.g. `WmsRequest(data_collection=...)`, with simple `dict` initializations, e.g. `dict(data_collection=...)`. Then somewhere during each testcase run you initialize e.g. `WmsRequest` with parameters from the dictionary. I noticed that such a solution has already been used [here](https://github.com/sentinel-hub/sentinelhub-py/blob/19b82df5cc27d31ed36cff212a84cdf00028b2e6/tests/test_geopedia.py#L95).

I know it will again be quite some work but I think it would significantly improve the testing procedure.

> Open questions:
> 1. Should `BSPCompileRequest` take multiple `FieldSet` values? This would allow the language backend to merge compilation via `CoarsenedTargets` since the ;language backend would see all of the relevant field sets together. I'm leaning toward that as the better approach than the current implementation in this PR.

In practice, I'm not sure that it will matter...? `CoarsenedTarget` is deterministic regardless of entrypoint, so if you have two files within one cycle, they will always "become" the same `CT`, and trigger the same compilation. As the API is now, that would mean two roots depending on the same inner compilation node.

Depending on how you're rendering outputs, merging might prevent redundant rendering of compilation outputs...? Our logging of `CompileResult` will already dedupe, but if you need to send specific per-request output, merging might make more sense.

> 2. Is there a way to get absolute paths into the Coursier cache? (So BSP can provide the scalac classpath to IntelliJ.) If not, then that resolution will need to be written out just like classfiles are currently by the PR.

There is. But I'm not sure that it's worth a special case. Note too that if BSP supports jars (rather than loose classfiles) as artifacts for that case, it would be surprising if it didn't support them for per-target compilation.

> 3. Big question is what should be in `classpath` returned by the `buildTarget/scalacOptions` BSP method. I'm going to ask on the intellij-scala Discord about it. At the very least, I am going to split the classfiles directories of different resolves. (And we will probably need to write out the jars of a resolve to a stable location just like for scalac classpath if there is no way to get paths into the Coursier cache that can be shared with IntelliJ, but that is not for this PR.)

See my question re: semantics below. I expect that the entire loaded "project" should be one resolve.

Really nice digging work around all the core functionality - I realised how hard it is to extract what the state machine does.
I remember you were unhappy about the special handling of the `ContractSendChannelBatchUnlock` - I can only mirror this sentiment. 

Regarding the updated code:

If I understand correctly, if there is no BP-mismatch, in the  `handle_contract_send_channelunlock() `the `restored_channel_state` is the same as the [initially retrieved](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/cfec408dadb7b242c82482677fc59fdc2e34f4a1/raiden/raiden_event_handler.py#L609) `channel_state`.
It would be easier to read when the edge-case of handling un-synced balance-proofs would be more explicit and trigger a different code-path than the normal case of handling the contract's `settle( )`.


Maybe we could initiate the code-path deviation already in the state-machine, since there we can check for mismatched balance-proofs.
Something vaguely like this:
```python 
 def handle_channel_settled( ):
 ...
    bp_mismatch = (
        channel_state.our_state.balance_proof.locksroot != our_locksroot
        or channel_state.partner_state.balance_proof.locksroot != partner_locksroot
    )
     if bp_mismatch:
        # Someone settled with an outdated locksroot, so now we have to retrieve an old channelstate
         return UpdateOldStateAndUnlock()
     else:
         return ContractSendChannelBatchUnlock( )
```
The `UpdateOldStateAndUnlock()` would then first update the `channel_state` for both end-states accordingly,
and then pipe this state to `handle_contract_send_channelunlock()` with the updated channel_state.

But this would mean quite some changes to the code.

@deundrewilliams  Your code change is totally fine, but I think our existing favorites tests are a little wonky, which is influencing the design of future tests. 

Right now, the setUp method [does some work](https://github.com/ucfopen/canvasapi/blob/271eccfeaf3bd537948d63b11098ab84510d9f52/tests/test_favorite.py#L26-L28) to get the current user and then set up a favorite course and a favorite group. This works for the existing tests because they’re only focused on [removing the favorite course and group](https://github.com/ucfopen/canvasapi/blob/develop/tests/test_favorite.py#L39-L53) and asserting that we consume the response to our DELETE request and return the same favorite object that we had before.

Unfortunately, the bug here is part of the interaction between the creation of our favorite objects and how we check to see which endpoint to call when we’re removing it. We need more control over the creation step under test, so we should probably pull it out of the `setUp` hook to provide it with its own fixture (the “return capitalized context codes instead of uncapitalized” one). If we do that, I think I’d like to modify our existing tests to decouple them. The good news is that there aren’t many of them.

Alternatively, I could see a place for a `RemoveFavoriteTestCase` where we do this kind of set up twice, providing those `setUp` methods twice with slightly different fixtures. I'm not sure how I feel about that though... seems like it might be less clear.

I think this is the wrong approach. Sorry. But I love that you've found a solution to the original problem. 
What I don't like is that it's very "oddly specific" and it swallows too many errors. 

Thing is if you use this lib with Memcache instead of Redis, the only acceptable way is to use your solution. It's also a bit weird that logging is suddenly introduced as part of the thread-safety problem. I would love to see logging added because I think it can help and if configured correctly, using the `logging` module, it's not a bad thing to have. What you should do is **first figure out if Memcached is the backend** and if so just use your `remember_url_cas()` without much "excuses or options". I was thinking something like this, inside `remember_url()`:

```python
def remember_url(self, request, cache_key, timeout):
    if self._is_memcache_backend():
        return self.remember_url_cas(url, cache_key)
    # If it's not Memcache, don't try to solve the thread-safety problem. 
    # TODO: Implement a solution for Redis 
    # See https://github.com/peterbe/django-fancy-cache/issues/7
    #
    # This is the default basic implementation which is not thread-safe.
    
     ... the old code ...

```

Regarding `_is_memcache_backend` perhaps we can do something like:
```python
hasattr(cache, 'make_key')
# or...
'memcached' settings.CACHES['default']['BACKEND']
```
I honestly don't know what is the best way to find out if `cache` is a proxy for Memcache. Experiment. 

And lastly, remove all `try:` and `except ...:` that aren't absolutely necessary.

Earlier, I argued that the connection coroutine should not use our optimized `Task` but the usual `asyncio.Task` (i.e. it should get started via `asyncio.ensure_future`). My argument then: we do not know the implementation details of the connection process (a loop responsibility); it is well possible that this requires a full blown `Task` implementation - and not our optimized but limited implementation. I had thought that I had convinced you then.

If you implement the solution above now, it will solve the current problem (because the first step of a standard task is "called soon" rather than immediately -- i.e. delayed until the loop is running) and you are safer regarding potential task related requirements by the connection process.

Sorry for the delay in looking at it, I’ve been swamped. I’ve only got one minor suggestion, though, for what it’s worth.

There are some high level things I would encourage doing beyond just this PR.  I'm happy to put in my own time towards them and handling them in a separate following PR seems ok.

1) Run the mypy checks at least across multiple platforms if not also across supported Python versions.  Dependencies can vary across each of those.  The watchdog code itself certainly varies a lot depending on the platform.

2) I tend to work with quite strict mypy settings.  Applications sort of get a choice but libraries need to live up to whatever standards they want to enable their users to achieve.  The default mypy setting mostly only checks stuff that is actively hinted.  Requiring that everything is hinted and that `Any` is banned as much as possible is valuable, along with misc other settings.  For the record, I look to https://github.com/altendky/qtrio/blob/459492f5f3d1fe6b6bb8b4e93d9a1bd1cdb4dc32/mypy.ini and https://github.com/Chia-Network/chia-blockchain/blob/8430b072e262cb7a80e688fdc3ad8f1fd2b50d0e/mypy.ini as my personal references for where I've ended up with mypy config.

3) I try to always ignore the specific error message as opposed to general `# type: ignore` comments.  I see that some ignores here do, many don't.  When following this, it is useful to add `show_error_codes = True` to `mypy.ini` so they error output includes the error code to ignore.

4) I certainly don't follow it in all my own cases...  but I do like having comments for type ignores.  This makes it easier to read and conclude they are appropriate.  If possible, a link to a PR or issue that when resolved should result in a reconsideration of the ignore.  Sometimes either some or all of the actual mypy complaint is good.

Again, thanks to all for the work here.  Based on my mypy checks failing with v2.3.1 but passing with this PR, this is an improvement.  Even if you like my suggestions, they can certainly be considered as followups for me to invest my own time in.  `:]`
